message.content:
The bell above the door jingled, cutting through the symphony of ticking that filled the shop. It wasn’t a single sound, but a layered rhythm—hundreds of clocks, each with its own heartbeat, creating a chaotic yet harmonious hum.

Leo, a boy of about eight with scraped knees and eyes wide as saucers, stepped inside. The air smelled of oil, old wood, and dust.

Behind a cluttered workbench sat Mr. Abernathy. He was so old he seemed to be made of the same leather and brass as the clocks around him. He wore a loupe over one eye, peering into the guts of a grandfather clock’s heart.

"Closed," Mr. Abernathy said, without looking up. His voice was like grinding gears.

"But the sign says 'Open,'" Leo insisted, pointing to the small chalkboard by the door.

Mr. Abernathy sighed, a sound like escaping steam. He set down his tweezers. "The sign is wrong. Time, however, is never wrong. Come in, then. But don’t touch anything. These aren’t toys; they are prisons for seconds."

Leo stepped closer, fascinated. He watched the pendulum of the grandfather clock swing back and forth. *Tick. Tock. Tick. Tock.*

"Why do they all go so fast?" Leo asked.

"Fast?" Mr. Abernathy chuckled dryly. "They go exactly as fast as they are supposed to. One second per second. It is the most honest thing in the world."

"But yesterday, when I was waiting for my birthday, the clock went so slow. And today, when I was playing, it went so fast. Did the clock break?"

Mr. Abernathy wiped his hands on a rag stained with grease. He looked at the boy
